question:
2. Show that the equation
\[
\left(9-x^{2}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=\left(9-y^{2}\right)
\]
characterizes a family of conics touching the four sides of a fixed square.
solution:
Solution. Equation (1) defines a two-valued direction field in the open square
\[
S:-3<x<3, \quad-3<y<3
\]
and in the four quadrants
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\( Q_{1}: \) & \( 3<x \), & \( 3<y \) \\
\( Q_{2}: \) & \( x<-3 \), & \( 3<y \) \\
\( Q_{3}: \) & \( x<-3 \), & \( y<-3 \) \\
\( Q_{4}: \) & \( 3<x \), & \( y<-3 \).
\end{tabular}

Let \( U \) be the union of these five open regions. The direction field can be extended continuously to the boundary of \( U \) except at the points \( ( \pm 3, \pm 3) \) of width 6 where no direction field is defined at all. We shall discuss the solutions of the differential equation first on the open region \( U \).
On \( U \) the two-valued direction field can be resolved into two ordinary direction fields given by the differential equations
(2)
\[
\frac{d y}{d x}=+\sqrt{\frac{9-y^{2}}{9-x^{2}}}
\]
(3)
\[
\frac{d y}{d x}=-\sqrt{\frac{9-y^{2}}{9-x^{2}}} .
\]

The right members of both of these equations are continuously differentiable (on \( U \) ), so there is a unique maximal solution for each equation through each
point of \( U \). These solutions can be found explicitly since the variables are separable. On \( S \), equation (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d y}{\sqrt{9-y^{2}}}=\frac{d x}{\sqrt{9-x^{2}}}
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\arcsin \frac{y}{3}=\arcsin \frac{x}{3}+C .
\]

Here \( C \) must be between \( -\pi \) and \( \pi \) because arcsin takes values between \( -\pi / 2 \) and \( \pi / 2 \). Similarly, equation (3) leads to
(5)
\[
\arcsin \frac{y}{3}=-\arcsin \frac{x}{3}+D
\]
on \( S \) with \( -\pi<D<\pi \).
On the quadrants (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d y}{\sqrt{y^{2}-9}}=\frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}-9}}
\]
giving
(6)
\( \operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|y|=\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|x|+E \),
where \( E \) may have any value. Equation (3) leads to
(7)
\[
\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|y|=-\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|x|+F .
\]

If we take the sine of both sides of (4) and use the addition formula we get
\[
\frac{y}{3}=\frac{x}{3} \cos C+\sqrt{1-\frac{x^{2}}{9}} \sin C .
\]

Clearing the radical, this reduces to
(8)
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x y \cos C=9 \sin ^{2} C .
\]

Similar transformations applied to (5), (6), and (7) give
(9)
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x y \cos D=9 \sin ^{2} D
\]
(10)
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}-2|x y| \cosh E=-9 \sinh ^{2} E,
\]
(11)
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+2|x y| \cosh F=-9 \sinh ^{2} F .
\]

Since \( |x y|=+x y \) or \( -x y \) throughout any single quadrant \( Q_{i} \), these equations show that the solution curves for all solutions of (2) and
the open domain \( U \) are parts of curves in the one parameter family
(12)
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}-2 \lambda x y=9\left(1-\lambda^{2}\right) .
\]

It follows from our work that there are at least two curves of the family (12) passing through each point of \( U \). There are, in fact, exactly two because, given \( (x, y) \), there will be exactly two values of \( \lambda \) satisfying (12) if and only if the discriminant of (12), regarded as a polynomial in \( \lambda \), is positive. This condition
if and only if \( (x, y) \in U \).
Except for the values \( \lambda= \pm 1 \) the curves (12) are conics tangent to the lines \( x= \pm 3 \) and \( y= \pm 3 \). The latter fact is easily seen; for example, if we put \( x=3 \) in (12) and solve for \( y \), we get a double root \( y=3 \lambda \). For \( \lambda= \pm 1 \), the conics degenerate into straight lines, \( (x-y)^{2}=0 \) and \( (x+y)^{2}=0 \). Equation (12) remains the same if we interchange \( x \) and \( y \) or \( -x \) and \( y \); hence, the conics are all symmetric in the lines \( y=x \) and \( y=-x \). For \( |\lambda|<1 \), the discriminant of (12) is negative, so dhe
are ellipses (a circle for \( \lambda=0 \) ) lying in \( \bar{S} \). For \( |\lambda|>1 \), the discriminant is positive and the curves are hyperbolas, each branch lying in one of the closed quadrants \( \overline{Q_{i}} \). (See Figure 1.)

Fig. 1. Some curves in the family (12).
The family (12) is, of course, the family of conics referred to in the problem. It is worth noting that we can derive the differential equation (1) directly from (12). Differentiating (12) implicitly we find
\[
\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x-\lambda y}{y-\lambda x}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2} & =\frac{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-2 \lambda x y\right)+\left(\lambda^{2}-1\right) y^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-2 \lambda x y\right)+\left(\lambda^{2}-1\right) x^{2}} \\
& =\frac{9\left(1-\lambda^{2}\right)+\left(\lambda^{2}-1\right) y^{2}}{9\left(1-\lambda^{2}\right)+\left(\lambda^{2}-1\right) x^{2}}=\frac{9-y^{2}}{9-x^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is essentially equivalent to (1).
Now we turn our attention to solutions of (1) on the closed region \( \bar{U} \). Because the right members of (2) and (3) do not represent functions differentiable on \( \bar{U} \), the uniqueness of solutions may and does fail at points of the boundary of \( U \). For example, \( y=3 \) and \( y=\sqrt{9-x^{2}} \) are both solutions of (1) passing through ( 0,3 ). We can piece together the old
solutions to (1) on \( U \) with various parts of the lines \( y= \pm 3 \) to obtain a solutions to (1) on \( U \) with various parts of the lines \( y= \pm 3 \) to obtain a great variety of solutions to (1). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
y & =\sqrt{9-x^{2}} \quad \text { for }-3<x<0 \\
& =3 \quad \text { for } 0 \leq x \leq 4 \frac{1}{2} \\
& =\frac{3}{2} x-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5\left(x^{2}-9\right)} \text { for } 4 \frac{1}{2}<x
\end{aligned}
\]
defines a solution to (1) whose graph includes a quadrant of a circle in \( S \) and part of a hyperbola (corresponding to \( \lambda=3 / 2 \) ) in \( Q_{1} \). (See Figure 2.)

Fig. 2.
If we interpret (1) as a differential equation for curves in the plane (including curves which may not be the graphs of functions), then the whole of any of the conics defined by (12) is a solution curve. In this interpretation we can also splice segments of the vertical lines \( x= \pm 3 \) into solutions obtaining curves that wander around in \( \bar{S} \) in a complicated
fashion, as shown in Figure 3

descriptive_long.question:
2. Show that the equation
\[
\left(9-abscissa^{2}\right)\left(\frac{d ordinate}{d abscissa}\right)^{2}=\left(9-ordinate^{2}\right)
\]
characterizes a family of conics touching the four sides of a fixed square.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Equation (1) defines a two-valued direction field in the open square
\[
regions:-3<abscissa<3, \quad-3<ordinate<3
\]
and in the four quadrants
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\( quadrantone: \) & \( 3<abscissa \), & \( 3<ordinate \) \\
\( quadranttwo: \) & \( abscissa<-3 \), & \( 3<ordinate \) \\
\( quadrantthree: \) & \( abscissa<-3 \), & \( ordinate<-3 \) \\
\( quadrantfour: \) & \( 3<abscissa \), & \( ordinate<-3 \).
\end{tabular}

Let \( regionu \) be the union of these five open regions. The direction field can be extended continuously to the boundary of \( regionu \) except at the points \( ( \pm 3, \pm 3) \) of width 6 where no direction field is defined at all. We shall discuss the solutions of the differential equation first on the open region \( regionu \).
On \( regionu \) the two-valued direction field can be resolved into two ordinary direction fields given by the differential equations
(2)
\[
\frac{d ordinate}{d abscissa}=+\sqrt{\frac{9-ordinate^{2}}{9-abscissa^{2}}}
\]
(3)
\[
\frac{d ordinate}{d abscissa}=-\sqrt{\frac{9-ordinate^{2}}{9-abscissa^{2}}} .
\]

The right members of both of these equations are continuously differentiable (on \( regionu \) ), so there is a unique maximal solution for each equation through each
point of \( regionu \). These solutions can be found explicitly since the variables are separable. On \( regions \), equation (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d ordinate}{\sqrt{9-ordinate^{2}}}=\frac{d abscissa}{\sqrt{9-abscissa^{2}}}
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\arcsin \frac{ordinate}{3}=\arcsin \frac{abscissa}{3}+constc .
\]

Here \( constc \) must be between \( -\pi \) and \( \pi \) because arcsin takes values between \( -\pi / 2 \) and \( \pi / 2 \). Similarly, equation (3) leads to
(5)
\[
\arcsin \frac{ordinate}{3}=-\arcsin \frac{abscissa}{3}+constd
\]
on \( regions \) with \( -\pi<constd<\pi \).
On the quadrants (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d ordinate}{\sqrt{ordinate^{2}-9}}=\frac{d abscissa}{\sqrt{abscissa^{2}-9}}
\]
giving
(6)
\( \operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|ordinate|=\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|abscissa|+conste \),
where \( conste \) may have any value. Equation (3) leads to
(7)
\[
\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|ordinate|=-\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|abscissa|+constf .
\]

If we take the sine of both sides of (4) and use the addition formula we get
\[
\frac{ordinate}{3}=\frac{abscissa}{3} \cos constc+\sqrt{1-\frac{abscissa^{2}}{9}} \sin constc .
\]

Clearing the radical, this reduces to
(8)
\[
abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}-2 abscissa ordinate \cos constc=9 \sin ^{2} constc .
\]

Similar transformations applied to (5), (6), and (7) give
(9)
\[
abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}+2 abscissa ordinate \cos constd=9 \sin ^{2} constd
\]
(10)
\[
abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}-2|abscissa ordinate| \cosh conste=-9 \sinh ^{2} conste,
\]
(11)
\[
abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}+2|abscissa ordinate| \cosh constf=-9 \sinh ^{2} constf .
\]

Since \( |abscissa ordinate|=+abscissa ordinate \) or \( -abscissa ordinate \) throughout any single quadrant \( quadrantgeneric \), these equations show that the solution curves for all solutions of (2) and
the open domain \( regionu \) are parts of curves in the one parameter family
(12)
\[
abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}-2 parameterlambda abscissa ordinate=9\left(1-parameterlambda^{2}\right) .
\]

It follows from our work that there are at least two curves of the family (12) passing through each point of \( regionu \). There are, in fact, exactly two because, given \( (abscissa, ordinate) \), there will be exactly two values of \( parameterlambda \) satisfying (12) if and only if the discriminant of (12), regarded as a polynomial in \( parameterlambda \), is positive. This condition
if and only if \( (abscissa, ordinate) \in regionu \).
Except for the values \( parameterlambda= \pm 1 \) the curves (12) are conics tangent to the lines \( abscissa= \pm 3 \) and \( ordinate= \pm 3 \). The latter fact is easily seen; for example, if we put \( abscissa=3 \) in (12) and solve for \( ordinate \), we get a double root \( ordinate=3 parameterlambda \). For \( parameterlambda= \pm 1 \), the conics degenerate into straight lines, \( (abscissa-ordinate)^{2}=0 \) and \( (abscissa+ordinate)^{2}=0 \). Equation (12) remains the same if we interchange \( abscissa \) and \( ordinate \) or \( -abscissa \) and \( ordinate \); hence, the conics are all symmetric in the lines \( ordinate=abscissa \) and \( ordinate=-abscissa \). For \( |parameterlambda|<1 \), the discriminant of (12) is negative, so dhe
are ellipses (a circle for \( parameterlambda=0 \) ) lying in \( \bar{regions} \). For \( |parameterlambda|>1 \), the discriminant is positive and the curves are hyperbolas, each branch lying in one of the closed quadrants \( \overline{quadrantgeneric} \). (See Figure 1.)

Fig. 1. Some curves in the family (12).
The family (12) is, of course, the family of conics referred to in the problem. It is worth noting that we can derive the differential equation (1) directly from (12). Differentiating (12) implicitly we find
\[
\frac{d ordinate}{d abscissa}=-\frac{abscissa-parameterlambda ordinate}{ordinate-parameterlambda abscissa}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d ordinate}{d abscissa}\right)^{2} & =\frac{\left(abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}-2 parameterlambda abscissa ordinate\right)+\left(parameterlambda^{2}-1\right) ordinate^{2}}{\left(abscissa^{2}+ordinate^{2}-2 parameterlambda abscissa ordinate\right)+\left(parameterlambda^{2}-1\right) abscissa^{2}} \\
& =\frac{9\left(1-parameterlambda^{2}\right)+\left(parameterlambda^{2}-1\right) ordinate^{2}}{9\left(1-parameterlambda^{2}\right)+\left(parameterlambda^{2}-1\right) abscissa^{2}}=\frac{9-ordinate^{2}}{9-abscissa^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is essentially equivalent to (1).
Now we turn our attention to solutions of (1) on the closed region \( \bar{regionu} \). Because the right members of (2) and (3) do not represent functions differentiable on \( \bar{regionu} \), the uniqueness of solutions may and does fail at points of the boundary of \( regionu \). For example, \( ordinate=3 \) and \( ordinate=\sqrt{9-abscissa^{2}} \) are both solutions of (1) passing through ( 0,3 ). We can piece together the old
solutions to (1) on \( regionu \) with various parts of the lines \( ordinate= \pm 3 \) to obtain a solutions to (1) on \( regionu \) with various parts of the lines \( ordinate= \pm 3 \) to obtain a great variety of solutions to (1). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
ordinate & =\sqrt{9-abscissa^{2}} \quad \text { for }-3<abscissa<0 \\
& =3 \quad \text { for } 0 \leq abscissa \leq 4 \frac{1}{2} \\
& =\frac{3}{2} abscissa-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5\left(abscissa^{2}-9\right)} \text { for } 4 \frac{1}{2}<abscissa
\end{aligned}
\]
defines a solution to (1) whose graph includes a quadrant of a circle in \( regions \) and part of a hyperbola (corresponding to \( parameterlambda=3 / 2 \) ) in \( quadrantone \). (See Figure 2.)

Fig. 2.
If we interpret (1) as a differential equation for curves in the plane (including curves which may not be the graphs of functions), then the whole of any of the conics defined by (12) is a solution curve. In this interpretation we can also splice segments of the vertical lines \( abscissa= \pm 3 \) into solutions obtaining curves that wander around in \( \bar{regions} \) in a complicated
fashion, as shown in Figure 3

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. Show that the equation
\[
\left(9-partridge^{2}\right)\left(\frac{d silkworm}{d partridge}\right)^{2}=\left(9-silkworm^{2}\right)
\]
characterizes a family of conics touching the four sides of a fixed square.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Equation (1) defines a two-valued direction field in the open square
\[
buttercup:-3<partridge<3, \quad-3<silkworm<3
\]
and in the four quadrants
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\( marshland: \) & \( 3<partridge \), & \( 3<silkworm \) \\
\( hintersea: \) & \( partridge<-3 \), & \( 3<silkworm \) \\
\( clifftide: \) & \( partridge<-3 \), & \( silkworm<-3 \) \\
\( pearlstone: \) & \( 3<partridge \), & \( silkworm<-3 \).
\end{tabular}

Let \( driftwood \) be the union of these five open regions. The direction field can be extended continuously to the boundary of \( driftwood \) except at the points \( ( \pm 3, \pm 3) \) of width 6 where no direction field is defined at all. We shall discuss the solutions of the differential equation first on the open region \( driftwood \).
On \( driftwood \) the two-valued direction field can be resolved into two ordinary direction fields given by the differential equations
(2)
\[
\frac{d silkworm}{d partridge}=+\sqrt{\frac{9-silkworm^{2}}{9-partridge^{2}}}
\]
(3)
\[
\frac{d silkworm}{d partridge}=-\sqrt{\frac{9-silkworm^{2}}{9-partridge^{2}}} .
\]

The right members of both of these equations are continuously differentiable (on \( driftwood \) ), so there is a unique maximal solution for each equation through each
point of \( driftwood \). These solutions can be found explicitly since the variables are separable. On \( buttercup \), equation (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d silkworm}{\sqrt{9-silkworm^{2}}}=\frac{d partridge}{\sqrt{9-partridge^{2}}}
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\arcsin \frac{silkworm}{3}=\arcsin \frac{partridge}{3}+teardrop .
\]

Here \( teardrop \) must be between \( -\pi \) and \( \pi \) because arcsin takes values between \( -\pi / 2 \) and \( \pi / 2 \). Similarly, equation (3) leads to
(5)
\[
\arcsin \frac{silkworm}{3}=-\arcsin \frac{partridge}{3}+snowflake
\]
on \( buttercup \) with \( -\pi<snowflake<\pi \).
On the quadrants (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d silkworm}{\sqrt{silkworm^{2}-9}}=\frac{d partridge}{\sqrt{partridge^{2}-9}}
\]
giving
(6)
\( \operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|silkworm|=\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|partridge|+carbuncle \),
where \( carbuncle \) may have any value. Equation (3) leads to
(7)
\[
\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|silkworm|=-\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|partridge|+moonlight .
\]

If we take the sine of both sides of (4) and use the addition formula we get
\[
\frac{silkworm}{3}=\frac{partridge}{3} \cos teardrop+\sqrt{1-\frac{partridge^{2}}{9}} \sin teardrop .
\]

Clearing the radical, this reduces to
(8)
\[
partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}-2 partridge silkworm \cos teardrop=9 \sin ^{2} teardrop .
\]

Similar transformations applied to (5), (6), and (7) give
(9)
\[
partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}+2 partridge silkworm \cos snowflake=9 \sin ^{2} snowflake
\]
(10)
\[
partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}-2|partridge silkworm| \cosh carbuncle=-9 \sinh ^{2} carbuncle,
\]
(11)
\[
partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}+2|partridge silkworm| \cosh moonlight=-9 \sinh ^{2} moonlight .
\]

Since \( |partridge silkworm|=+partridge silkworm \) or \( -partridge silkworm \) throughout any single quadrant \( meadowlark \), these equations show that the solution curves for all solutions of (2) and
the open domain \( driftwood \) are parts of curves in the one parameter family
(12)
\[
partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}-2 corkscrew partridge silkworm=9\left(1-corkscrew^{2}\right) .
\]

It follows from our work that there are at least two curves of the family (12) passing through each point of \( driftwood \). There are, in fact, exactly two because, given \( (partridge, silkworm) \), there will be exactly two values of \( corkscrew \) satisfying (12) if and only if the discriminant of (12), regarded as a polynomial in \( corkscrew \), is positive. This condition
if and only if \( (partridge, silkworm) \in driftwood \).
Except for the values \( corkscrew= \pm 1 \) the curves (12) are conics tangent to the lines \( partridge= \pm 3 \) and \( silkworm= \pm 3 \). The latter fact is easily seen; for example, if we put \( partridge=3 \) in (12) and solve for \( silkworm \), we get a double root \( silkworm=3 corkscrew \). For \( corkscrew= \pm 1 \), the conics degenerate into straight lines, \( (partridge-silkworm)^{2}=0 \) and \( (partridge+silkworm)^{2}=0 \). Equation (12) remains the same if we interchange \( partridge \) and \( silkworm \) or \( -partridge \) and \( silkworm \); hence, the conics are all symmetric in the lines \( silkworm=partridge \) and \( silkworm=-partridge \). For \( |corkscrew|<1 \), the discriminant of (12) is negative, so dhe
are ellipses (a circle for \( corkscrew=0 \) ) lying in \( \bar{buttercup} \). For \( |corkscrew|>1 \), the discriminant is positive and the curves are hyperbolas, each branch lying in one of the closed quadrants \( \overline{meadowlark} \). (See Figure 1.)

Fig. 1. Some curves in the family (12).
The family (12) is, of course, the family of conics referred to in the problem. It is worth noting that we can derive the differential equation (1) directly from (12). Differentiating (12) implicitly we find
\[
\frac{d silkworm}{d partridge}=-\frac{partridge-corkscrew silkworm}{silkworm-corkscrew partridge}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d silkworm}{d partridge}\right)^{2} & =\frac{\left(partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}-2 corkscrew partridge silkworm\right)+\left(corkscrew^{2}-1\right) silkworm^{2}}{\left(partridge^{2}+silkworm^{2}-2 corkscrew partridge silkworm\right)+\left(corkscrew^{2}-1\right) partridge^{2}} \\
& =\frac{9\left(1-corkscrew^{2}\right)+\left(corkscrew^{2}-1\right) silkworm^{2}}{9\left(1-corkscrew^{2}\right)+\left(corkscrew^{2}-1\right) partridge^{2}}=\frac{9-silkworm^{2}}{9-partridge^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is essentially equivalent to (1).
Now we turn our attention to solutions of (1) on the closed region \( \bar{driftwood} \). Because the right members of (2) and (3) do not represent functions differentiable on \( \bar{driftwood} \), the uniqueness of solutions may and does fail at points of the boundary of \( driftwood \). For example, \( silkworm=3 \) and \( silkworm=\sqrt{9-partridge^{2}} \) are both solutions of (1) passing through ( 0,3 ). We can piece together the old
solutions to (1) on \( driftwood \) with various parts of the lines \( silkworm= \pm 3 \) to obtain a solutions to (1) on \( driftwood \) with various parts of the lines \( silkworm= \pm 3 \) to obtain a great variety of solutions to (1). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
silkworm & =\sqrt{9-partridge^{2}} \quad \text { for }-3<partridge<0 \\
& =3 \quad \text { for } 0 \leq partridge \leq 4 \frac{1}{2} \\
& =\frac{3}{2} partridge-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5\left(partridge^{2}-9\right)} \text { for } 4 \frac{1}{2}<partridge
\end{aligned}
\]
defines a solution to (1) whose graph includes a quadrant of a circle in \( buttercup \) and part of a hyperbola (corresponding to \( corkscrew=3 / 2 \) ) in \( marshland \). (See Figure 2.)

Fig. 2.
If we interpret (1) as a differential equation for curves in the plane (including curves which may not be the graphs of functions), then the whole of any of the conics defined by (12) is a solution curve. In this interpretation we can also splice segments of the vertical lines \( partridge= \pm 3 \) into solutions obtaining curves that wander around in \( \bar{buttercup} \) in a complicated
fashion, as shown in Figure 3

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. Show that the equation
\[
\left(9-verticalaxis^{2}\right)\left(\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}\right)^{2}=\left(9-horizontalaxis^{2}\right)
\]
characterizes a family of conics touching the four sides of a fixed square.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Equation (1) defines a two-valued direction field in the open square
\[
circleregion:-3<verticalaxis<3, \quad-3<horizontalaxis<3
\]
and in the four quadrants
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\( halfplanealpha: \) & \( 3<verticalaxis \), & \( 3<horizontalaxis \) \\
\( halfplanebeta: \) & \( verticalaxis<-3 \), & \( 3<horizontalaxis \) \\
\( halfplanegamma: \) & \( verticalaxis<-3 \), & \( horizontalaxis<-3 \) \\
\( halfplanedelta: \) & \( 3<verticalaxis \), & \( horizontalaxis<-3 \).
\end{tabular}

Let \( intersection \) be the union of these five open regions. The direction field can be extended continuously to the boundary of \( intersection \) except at the points \( ( \pm 3, \pm 3) \) of width 6 where no direction field is defined at all. We shall discuss the solutions of the differential equation first on the open region \( intersection \).
On \( intersection \) the two-valued direction field can be resolved into two ordinary direction fields given by the differential equations
(2)
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}=+\sqrt{\frac{9-horizontalaxis^{2}}{9-verticalaxis^{2}}}
\]
(3)
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}=-\sqrt{\frac{9-horizontalaxis^{2}}{9-verticalaxis^{2}}} .
\]

The right members of both of these equations are continuously differentiable (on \( intersection \) ), so there is a unique maximal solution for each equation through each
point of \( intersection \). These solutions can be found explicitly since the variables are separable. On \( circleregion \), equation (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{\sqrt{9-horizontalaxis^{2}}}=\frac{d verticalaxis}{\sqrt{9-verticalaxis^{2}}}
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\arcsin \frac{horizontalaxis}{3}=\arcsin \frac{verticalaxis}{3}+variability .
\]

Here \( variability \) must be between \( -\pi \) and \( \pi \) because arcsin takes values between \( -\pi / 2 \) and \( \pi / 2 \). Similarly, equation (3) leads to
(5)
\[
\arcsin \frac{horizontalaxis}{3}=-\arcsin \frac{verticalaxis}{3}+fluctuation
\]
on \( circleregion \) with \( -\pi<fluctuation<\pi \).
On the quadrants (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{\sqrt{horizontalaxis^{2}-9}}=\frac{d verticalaxis}{\sqrt{verticalaxis^{2}-9}}
\]
giving
(6)
\( \operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|horizontalaxis|=\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|verticalaxis|+alteration \),
where \( alteration \) may have any value. Equation (3) leads to
(7)
\[
\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|horizontalaxis|=-\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|verticalaxis|+modification .
\]

If we take the sine of both sides of (4) and use the addition formula we get
\[
\frac{horizontalaxis}{3}=\frac{verticalaxis}{3} \cos variability+\sqrt{1-\frac{verticalaxis^{2}}{9}} \sin variability .
\]

Clearing the radical, this reduces to
(8)
\[
verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}-2 verticalaxis horizontalaxis \cos variability=9 \sin ^{2} variability .
\]

Similar transformations applied to (5), (6), and (7) give
(9)
\[
verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}+2 verticalaxis horizontalaxis \cos fluctuation=9 \sin ^{2} fluctuation
\]
(10)
\[
verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}-2|verticalaxis horizontalaxis| \cosh alteration=-9 \sinh ^{2} alteration,
\]
(11)
\[
verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}+2|verticalaxis horizontalaxis| \cosh modification=-9 \sinh ^{2} modification .
\]

Since \( |verticalaxis horizontalaxis|=+verticalaxis horizontalaxis \) or \( -verticalaxis horizontalaxis \) throughout any single quadrant \( halfplaneiota \), these equations show that the solution curves for all solutions of (2) and
the open domain \( intersection \) are parts of curves in the one parameter family
(12)
\[
verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}-2 independence verticalaxis horizontalaxis=9\left(1-independence^{2}\right) .
\]

It follows from our work that there are at least two curves of the family (12) passing through each point of \( intersection \). There are, in fact, exactly two because, given \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \), there will be exactly two values of \( independence \) satisfying (12) if and only if the discriminant of (12), regarded as a polynomial in \( independence \), is positive. This condition
if and only if \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \in intersection \).
Except for the values \( independence= \pm 1 \) the curves (12) are conics tangent to the lines \( verticalaxis= \pm 3 \) and \( horizontalaxis= \pm 3 \). The latter fact is easily seen; for example, if we put \( verticalaxis=3 \) in (12) and solve for \( horizontalaxis \), we get a double root \( horizontalaxis=3 independence \). For \( independence= \pm 1 \), the conics degenerate into straight lines, \( (verticalaxis-horizontalaxis)^{2}=0 \) and \( (verticalaxis+horizontalaxis)^{2}=0 \). Equation (12) remains the same if we interchange \( verticalaxis \) and \( horizontalaxis \) or \( -verticalaxis \) and \( horizontalaxis \); hence, the conics are all symmetric in the lines \( horizontalaxis=verticalaxis \) and \( horizontalaxis=-verticalaxis \). For \( |independence|<1 \), the discriminant of (12) is negative, so dhe
are ellipses (a circle for \( independence=0 \) ) lying in \( \bar{circleregion} \). For \( |independence|>1 \), the discriminant is positive and the curves are hyperbolas, each branch lying in one of the closed quadrants \( \overline{halfplaneiota} \). (See Figure 1.)

Fig. 1. Some curves in the family (12).
The family (12) is, of course, the family of conics referred to in the problem. It is worth noting that we can derive the differential equation (1) directly from (12). Differentiating (12) implicitly we find
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}=-\frac{verticalaxis-independence horizontalaxis}{horizontalaxis-independence verticalaxis}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}\right)^{2} & =\frac{\left(verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}-2 independence verticalaxis horizontalaxis\right)+\left(independence^{2}-1\right) horizontalaxis^{2}}{\left(verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}-2 independence verticalaxis horizontalaxis\right)+\left(independence^{2}-1\right) verticalaxis^{2}} \\
& =\frac{9\left(1-independence^{2}\right)+\left(independence^{2}-1\right) horizontalaxis^{2}}{9\left(1-independence^{2}\right)+\left(independence^{2}-1\right) verticalaxis^{2}}=\frac{9-horizontalaxis^{2}}{9-verticalaxis^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is essentially equivalent to (1).
Now we turn our attention to solutions of (1) on the closed region \( \bar{intersection} \). Because the right members of (2) and (3) do not represent functions differentiable on \( \bar{intersection} \), the uniqueness of solutions may and does fail at points of the boundary of \( intersection \). For example, \( horizontalaxis=3 \) and \( horizontalaxis=\sqrt{9-verticalaxis^{2}} \) are both solutions of (1) passing through ( 0,3 ). We can piece together the old
solutions to (1) on \( intersection \) with various parts of the lines \( horizontalaxis= \pm 3 \) to obtain a solutions to (1) on \( intersection \) with various parts of the lines \( horizontalaxis= \pm 3 \) to obtain a great variety of solutions to (1). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
horizontalaxis & =\sqrt{9-verticalaxis^{2}} \quad \text { for }-3<verticalaxis<0 \\
& =3 \quad \text { for } 0 \leq verticalaxis \leq 4 \frac{1}{2} \\
& =\frac{3}{2} verticalaxis-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5\left(verticalaxis^{2}-9\right)} \text { for } 4 \frac{1}{2}<verticalaxis
\end{aligned}
\]
defines a solution to (1) whose graph includes a quadrant of a circle in \( circleregion \) and part of a hyperbola (corresponding to \( independence=3 / 2 \) ) in \( halfplanealpha \). (See Figure 2.)

Fig. 2.
If we interpret (1) as a differential equation for curves in the plane (including curves which may not be the graphs of functions), then the whole of any of the conics defined by (12) is a solution curve. In this interpretation we can also splice segments of the vertical lines \( verticalaxis= \pm 3 \) into solutions obtaining curves that wander around in \( \bar{circleregion} \) in a complicated
fashion, as shown in Figure 3

garbled_string.question:
2. Show that the equation
\[
\left(9-gdwsnrmk^{2}\right)\left(\frac{d plkzqtnf}{d gdwsnrmk}\right)^{2}=\left(9-plkzqtnf^{2}\right)
\]
characterizes a family of conics touching the four sides of a fixed square.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Equation (1) defines a two-valued direction field in the open square
\[
hxlsbnvo:-3<gdwsnrmk<3, \quad-3<plkzqtnf<3
\]
and in the four quadrants
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\( uwyektbp: \) & \( 3<gdwsnrmk \), & \( 3<plkzqtnf \) \\
\( nqxwhfsl: \) & \( gdwsnrmk<-3 \), & \( 3<plkzqtnf \) \\
\( fyhzkdjo: \) & \( gdwsnrmk<-3 \), & \( plkzqtnf<-3 \) \\
\( kpsrwjzu: \) & \( 3<gdwsnrmk \), & \( plkzqtnf<-3 \).
\end{tabular}

Let \( oqprytnc \) be the union of these five open regions. The direction field can be extended continuously to the boundary of \( oqprytnc \) except at the points \( ( \pm 3, \pm 3) \) of width 6 where no direction field is defined at all. We shall discuss the solutions of the differential equation first on the open region \( oqprytnc \).
On \( oqprytnc \) the two-valued direction field can be resolved into two ordinary direction fields given by the differential equations
(2)
\[
\frac{d plkzqtnf}{d gdwsnrmk}=+\sqrt{\frac{9-plkzqtnf^{2}}{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}}}
\]
(3)
\[
\frac{d plkzqtnf}{d gdwsnrmk}=-\sqrt{\frac{9-plkzqtnf^{2}}{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}}} .
\]

The right members of both of these equations are continuously differentiable (on \( oqprytnc \) ), so there is a unique maximal solution for each equation through each
point of \( oqprytnc \). These solutions can be found explicitly since the variables are separable. On \( hxlsbnvo \), equation (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d plkzqtnf}{\sqrt{9-plkzqtnf^{2}}}=\frac{d gdwsnrmk}{\sqrt{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}}}
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\arcsin \frac{plkzqtnf}{3}=\arcsin \frac{gdwsnrmk}{3}+rvdhbqps .
\]

Here \( rvdhbqps \) must be between \( -\pi \) and \( \pi \) because arcsin takes values between \( -\pi / 2 \) and \( \pi / 2 \). Similarly, equation (3) leads to
(5)
\[
\arcsin \frac{plkzqtnf}{3}=-\arcsin \frac{gdwsnrmk}{3}+mbcztlxa
\]
on \( hxlsbnvo \) with \( -\pi<mbcztlxa<\pi \).
On the quadrants (2) becomes
\[
\frac{d plkzqtnf}{\sqrt{plkzqtnf^{2}-9}}=\frac{d gdwsnrmk}{\sqrt{gdwsnrmk^{2}-9}}
\]
giving
(6)
\( \operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|plkzqtnf|=\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|gdwsnrmk|+sjkhanwr \),
where \( sjkhanwr \) may have any value. Equation (3) leads to
(7)
\[
\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|plkzqtnf|=-\operatorname{arccosh} \frac{1}{3}|gdwsnrmk|+vmqsdzto .
\]

If we take the sine of both sides of (4) and use the addition formula we get
\[
\frac{plkzqtnf}{3}=\frac{gdwsnrmk}{3} \cos rvdhbqps+\sqrt{1-\frac{gdwsnrmk^{2}}{9}} \sin rvdhbqps .
\]

Clearing the radical, this reduces to
(8)
\[
gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}-2 gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf \cos rvdhbqps=9 \sin ^{2} rvdhbqps .
\]

Similar transformations applied to (5), (6), and (7) give
(9)
\[
gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}+2 gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf \cos mbcztlxa=9 \sin ^{2} mbcztlxa
\]
(10)
\[
gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}-2|gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf| \cosh sjkhanwr=-9 \sinh ^{2} sjkhanwr,
\]
(11)
\[
gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}+2|gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf| \cosh vmqsdzto=-9 \sinh ^{2} vmqsdzto .
\]

Since \( |gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf|=+gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf \) or \( -gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf \) throughout any single quadrant \( zrlmpcne \), these equations show that the solution curves for all solutions of (2) and
the open domain \( oqprytnc \) are parts of curves in the one parameter family
(12)
\[
gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}-2 ieowazsv gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf=9\left(1-ieowazsv^{2}\right) .
\]

It follows from our work that there are at least two curves of the family (12) passing through each point of \( oqprytnc \). There are, in fact, exactly two because, given \( (gdwsnrmk, plkzqtnf) \), there will be exactly two values of \( ieowazsv \) satisfying (12) if and only if the discriminant of (12), regarded as a polynomial in \( ieowazsv \), is positive. This condition
if and only if \( (gdwsnrmk, plkzqtnf) \in oqprytnc \).
Except for the values \( ieowazsv= \pm 1 \) the curves (12) are conics tangent to the lines \( gdwsnrmk= \pm 3 \) and \( plkzqtnf= \pm 3 \). The latter fact is easily seen; for example, if we put \( gdwsnrmk=3 \) in (12) and solve for \( plkzqtnf \), we get a double root \( plkzqtnf=3 ieowazsv \). For \( ieowazsv= \pm 1 \), the conics degenerate into straight lines, \( (gdwsnrmk-plkzqtnf)^{2}=0 \) and \( (gdwsnrmk+plkzqtnf)^{2}=0 \). Equation (12) remains the same if we interchange \( gdwsnrmk \) and \( plkzqtnf \) or \( -gdwsnrmk \) and \( plkzqtnf \); hence, the conics are all symmetric in the lines \( plkzqtnf=gdwsnrmk \) and \( plkzqtnf=-gdwsnrmk \). For \( |ieowazsv|<1 \), the discriminant of (12) is negative, so dhe
are ellipses (a circle for \( ieowazsv=0 \) ) lying in \( \bar{hxlsbnvo} \). For \( |ieowazsv|>1 \), the discriminant is positive and the curves are hyperbolas, each branch lying in one of the closed quadrants \( \overline{zrlmpcne} \). (See Figure 1.)

Fig. 1. Some curves in the family (12).
The family (12) is, of course, the family of conics referred to in the problem. It is worth noting that we can derive the differential equation (1) directly from (12). Differentiating (12) implicitly we find
\[
\frac{d plkzqtnf}{d gdwsnrmk}=-\frac{gdwsnrmk-ieowazsv plkzqtnf}{plkzqtnf-ieowazsv gdwsnrmk}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d plkzqtnf}{d gdwsnrmk}\right)^{2} & =\frac{\left(gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}-2 ieowazsv gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf\right)+\left(ieowazsv^{2}-1\right) plkzqtnf^{2}}{\left(gdwsnrmk^{2}+plkzqtnf^{2}-2 ieowazsv gdwsnrmk plkzqtnf\right)+\left(ieowazsv^{2}-1\right) gdwsnrmk^{2}} \\
& =\frac{9\left(1-ieowazsv^{2}\right)+\left(ieowazsv^{2}-1\right) plkzqtnf^{2}}{9\left(1-ieowazsv^{2}\right)+\left(ieowazsv^{2}-1\right) gdwsnrmk^{2}}=\frac{9-plkzqtnf^{2}}{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is essentially equivalent to (1).
Now we turn our attention to solutions of (1) on the closed region \( \bar{oqprytnc} \). Because the right members of (2) and (3) do not represent functions differentiable on \( \bar{oqprytnc} \), the uniqueness of solutions may and does fail at points of the boundary of \( oqprytnc \). For example, \( plkzqtnf=3 \) and \( plkzqtnf=\sqrt{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}} \) are both solutions of (1) passing through ( 0,3 ). We can piece together the old
solutions to (1) on \( oqprytnc \) with various parts of the lines \( plkzqtnf= \pm 3 \) to obtain a solutions to (1) on \( oqprytnc \) with various parts of the lines \( plkzqtnf= \pm 3 \) to obtain a great variety of solutions to (1). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
plkzqtnf & =\sqrt{9-gdwsnrmk^{2}} \quad \text { for }-3<gdwsnrmk<0 \\
& =3 \quad \text { for } 0 \leq gdwsnrmk \leq 4 \frac{1}{2} \\
& =\frac{3}{2} gdwsnrmk-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5\left(gdwsnrmk^{2}-9\right)} \text { for } 4 \frac{1}{2}<gdwsnrmk
\end{aligned}
\]
defines a solution to (1) whose graph includes a quadrant of a circle in \( hxlsbnvo \) and part of a hyperbola (corresponding to \( ieowazsv=3 / 2 \) ) in \( uwyektbp \). (See Figure 2.)

Fig. 2.
If we interpret (1) as a differential equation for curves in the plane (including curves which may not be the graphs of functions), then the whole of any of the conics defined by (12) is a solution curve. In this interpretation we can also splice segments of the vertical lines \( gdwsnrmk= \pm 3 \) into solutions obtaining curves that wander around in \( \bar{hxlsbnvo} \) in a complicated
fashion, as shown in Figure 3

kernel_variant.question:
Let a square of side-length 10 be centred at the origin, so that its sides are the four lines x = \pm 5 and y = \pm 5.

Show that the differential equation
\[
(25-x^{2})\Bigl(\tfrac{dy}{dx}\Bigr)^{2}=25-y^{2}\tag{\*}
\]
characterises exactly the one-parameter family of conics that are tangent to all four sides of that square.
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Splitting the two-valued direction field
Because the right-hand side of (*) becomes negative when |x|>5 or |y|>5, the differential equation makes sense in the open square
S: -5 < x < 5 ,   -5 < y < 5
and in the four exterior quadrants
Q_1 : 5 < x ,  5 < y ;   Q_2 : x < -5 , 5 < y ;
Q_3 : x < -5 , y < -5 ;  Q_4 : 5 < x , y < -5.
On each of these regions the equation yields the two ordinary differential equations
    dy/dx = \pm \sqrt{(25-y^2)/(25-x^2)}.

2.  Separation of variables and first integrals
(a)  Inside the square S:
    dy/\sqrt{25-y^2} = \pm  dx/\sqrt{25-x^2}  \Rightarrow   arcsin(y/5) = \pm  arcsin(x/5) + C.  (2)
(b)  In any exterior quadrant Q_i, both 25-x^2<0 and 25-y^2<0, so writing the real roots gives
    dy/\sqrt{y^2-25} = \pm  dx/\sqrt{x^2-25}  \Rightarrow   arccosh(|y|/5) = \pm  arccosh(|x|/5) + C'.  (3)

3.  Removing the radicals
From (2) with the plus sign and the addition formula for sin(\alpha +\beta ) we obtain
    y/5 = (x/5) cos C + \sqrt{1-x^2/25} sin C.
Squaring and clearing the remaining radical yields
    x^2 + y^2 - 2xy cos C = 25 sin^2 C.  (4)
Doing the same for the minus choice in (2) and for both choices in (3) produces in every case an equation of the form
    x^2 + y^2 - 2\lambda  xy = 25 (1-\lambda ^2),  (5)
where \lambda  = cos C (|\lambda |\leq 1) in the interior and \lambda  = \pm cosh C' (|\lambda |\geq 1) in an exterior quadrant.

4.  Geometric description
Equation (5) is a one-parameter family of conics symmetric in the lines y=x and y=-x.
*  If |\lambda |<1 the discriminant 4(\lambda ^2-1)<0, so we get ellipses (a circle when \lambda =0) lying entirely in the closed square.
*  If |\lambda |>1 the discriminant is positive and we obtain hyperbolas whose two branches lie in the closed exterior quadrants.
*  When \lambda =\pm 1 it degenerates to (x\mp y)^2=0, the diagonals.

Tangency to the sides is immediate: setting x=5 in (5) gives
    25 + y^2 - 10\lambda y = 25(1-\lambda ^2)
\Leftrightarrow  (y-5\lambda )^2 = 0,
so each conic is tangent to x=5 at (5,5\lambda ), and by symmetry to x=-5 and y=\pm 5.

5.  Necessity
Every solution of (*) must satisfy one of the integrated forms (4) or its analogues, hence ultimately (5).  Thus every maximal integral curve lies on one of the conics (5).

6.  Sufficiency
Differentiating (5) implicitly yields
    dy/dx = -(x-\lambda y)/(y-\lambda x),
so
  (dy/dx)^2 = (x-\lambda y)^2/(y-\lambda x)^2
           = [ (x^2+y^2-2\lambda xy)+(\lambda ^2-1)y^2 ]
             /[ (x^2+y^2-2\lambda xy)+(\lambda ^2-1)x^2 ].
Substituting x^2+y^2-2\lambda xy = 25(1-\lambda ^2) from (5) gives
    (dy/dx)^2 = (25-y^2)/(25-x^2),
which is exactly (*).

7.  Conclusion
The integral curves of (25-x^2)(dy/dx)^2 = 25-y^2 are precisely the conics
    x^2 + y^2 - 2\lambda  xy = 25(1-\lambda ^2),  (\lambda \in \mathbb{R}),
each tangent to the four lines x=\pm 5, y=\pm 5, and conversely every such conic satisfies the differential equation.

core_steps: Split ±-valued differential equation into dy/dx = ±√((a²−y²)/(a²−x²)) and separate variables
Integrate to obtain inverse‐trig / inverse‐hyperbolic relations (arcsin/arccosh) between x and y plus a constant
Apply addition formulas to eliminate radicals and derive quadratic form x² + y² − 2λxy = a²(1−λ²)
Recognize this quadratic as a one-parameter family of conics and check tangency to the lines x = ±a, y = ±a
Verify that every such conic satisfies the original differential equation, completing the characterization

slot1.description: Half–side length of the square; appears as ±3 in domain bounds, in arguments y/3 , x/3 of arcsin, and in tangency lines x=±3, y=±3

slot2.description: Square of the half–side length; appears as constant 9 multiplying terms in the ODE and on the right side of the conic equation
